Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like I absolutely had to, usually late at night when things got quiet. With 12 online courses, I just kind of bounced between whatever seemed most urgent. I'd spend maybe 9 hours a week total, spread thin across everything. It wasn't organized at all—more like putting out fires constantly.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place depending on what was due. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd cram everything into a couple of days. Deadlines were really the only thing that got me moving. I wanted to be more consistent, but between everything going on and feeling kind of down, regular schedules just didn't stick for me.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or getting an email notification that something was due. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I was missing anything major. It wasn't like I woke up excited to see what was on the LMS—it was more like checking to make sure I wasn't failing. Occasionally I'd log in and just stare at the dashboard feeling overwhelmed.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never felt like a natural part of my day. There were brief periods where I tried to check in daily, but that fell apart quickly. With low resources and limited time, the platform always felt like another thing demanding my attention rather than something helpful. It stayed more of an obligation than a habit.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful to me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and read them on my own time without needing constant internet. The assignment guidelines were helpful too because at least I knew what was expected. Discussion posts sometimes helped me understand concepts when other students explained things differently than the lectures.
Q6: I mostly ignored the videos—I watched some, but over 200 minutes feels like a lot and I often couldn't focus through them. Optional readings and extra resources got skipped because I was just trying to survive the required stuff. Anything that felt like it would take too long without directly helping my grade got pushed aside. I know that's not ideal, but I had to prioritize.
Q7: If I could see a direct connection between the activity and my assignment or exam, it felt worth doing. Anything that seemed like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my grade felt like a waste of limited energy. When I was already feeling sad and stretched thin, I needed activities to prove their value quickly. Clear instructions and obvious relevance made all the difference.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done because they had to, but optional stuff felt like extra weight when I was already struggling to keep up. There was always this guilt about not doing them, but also this exhaustion that made me think 'what's the point?' Required activities had deadlines and consequences, which honestly was the only thing motivating me some days.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded almost everything I could and studied from those files offline. I'd print readings when I could access a printer, take handwritten notes, and review those instead of logging back into the LMS. With limited resources and inconsistent internet sometimes, working offline was just more practical. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the screen.
Q10: There were many nights where I'd be going through my printed notes or downloaded PDFs for hours, but the LMS would show nothing. I'd reread materials multiple times trying to make things stick, all offline. Sometimes I'd discuss concepts with a friend over the phone, which helped me understand things but left no digital trace. My real study time was often invisible.
Q11: Yes, this was basically my standard approach. I'd access something once, download it, and then never open it on the LMS again. The PDFs lived on my laptop and I'd study from there repeatedly. It saved data and meant I didn't need to be online constantly. So my single login to grab materials turned into hours of actual study time that nobody would see recorded.
Q12: Around midterms, everything felt impossible. I was dealing with this persistent sadness that made getting out of bed hard, let alone engaging with coursework. The CBT sessions I had helped somewhat, but there were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care. When you're feeling low, even logging into a website feels like climbing a mountain. Those were the times my attendance really dropped.
Q13: Definitely. The workload across 12 courses was overwhelming, and when I was tired or confused, the LMS became something I avoided. If I didn't understand something, I'd close the tab rather than struggle through it. Competing responsibilities—work stuff, personal issues—meant the LMS got whatever scraps of energy I had left. Fatigue made everything feel harder than it probably was.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have the LMS open, scrolling through materials, but my mind was somewhere else entirely. Sometimes I'd watch a video but realize at the end I hadn't absorbed anything. Being physically present on the platform didn't mean I was mentally there. When you're sad, you can go through motions without actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly even though I knew participation mattered. They felt socially demanding in a way that exhausted me. I'd also avoid starting assignments until the last minute because the thought of beginning was somehow worse than the stress of rushing. It's like my brain preferred future-me's panic over present-me's effort. The avoidance made everything worse but I kept doing it.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only reason I engaged at all. I'd see something was due in two days and suddenly find the motivation that had been missing for weeks. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically right before due dates and flatlined otherwise. It's not healthy, but the pressure of a deadline cut through the fog in a way nothing else did. Without deadlines, I might not have done anything.
Q17: Feedback helped when I actually got it in time to use it. Good feedback on an assignment made me feel like maybe I wasn't completely failing, which lifted my mood a bit. But if feedback came too late or was vague, it just added to the sense that nothing I did mattered. The CBT sessions taught me to look for evidence against negative thoughts, and positive feedback served that purpose sometimes.
Q18: The structure felt loose enough that I could drift, which was both good and bad. Good because I could work around my inconsistent energy levels, bad because there was nothing forcing me to stay on track. Having 12 courses meant the structure of any single one got lost in the chaos. More check-ins or mandatory milestones might have helped me stay engaged, even if I would have complained about them.
Q19: The reading-heavy format actually suited my learning style, which helped. But the sheer volume of content across all my courses made engagement harder—I couldn't tell what was essential versus supplementary. Clearer prioritization of materials would have helped me focus my limited energy. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, which made me engage less authentically than I might have otherwise.
Q20: I think sometimes people are trying to figure out what they need to do without actually doing it yet. They might be anxious and checking things repeatedly, or logging in hoping motivation will strike. High activity could also be someone who's confused and searching for answers without finding them. It doesn't always mean productive engagement—sometimes it's just digital wandering.
Q21: That's pretty much me. My LMS activity was low, but I still got a Merit and decent scores because I studied effectively offline. Once I understood what was needed, I didn't need to keep returning to the platform. Some people are efficient—they get what they need and work with it elsewhere. Low logins don't mean low effort, just different study habits.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone logs in, what they click on, time spent on pages. But they completely miss the quality of that time and everything that happens offline. They can't capture whether someone was actually thinking or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss emotional states, external circumstances, and all the studying that happens with downloaded materials or physical notes.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, when concepts stick with you beyond the exam. I wasn't always engaged this semester—sometimes I was just surviving. Real engagement feels different from going through motions, and honestly, I experienced more of the latter. The sadness made genuine engagement rare.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how the CBT sessions were the only thing keeping me somewhat functional. It wouldn't show the hours spent with downloaded PDFs, the conversations with friends about course content, or the mental energy it took just to submit assignments. It definitely wouldn't capture that getting a Merit while feeling this low actually represents significant effort, even if my activity looks minimal.
